Comparing against θ = ∞ (uniform weighting), the F-test yields F = ", round(F_stat_distance, 1), " (p = ", round(p_distance, 3), "), confirming that spatial decay significantly improves fit. Diffusion is spatially localized: protests in nearby districts matter substantially more than those far away. At θ = ", theta_hat, " km, a district 200 km away exerts ", round(100*exp(-200/theta_hat)), "% as much influence as an adjacent district."))`

```{r figure-spatial-kernel, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Estimated spatial decay kernel. The curve shows how the weight on protests in other districts declines with geographic distance.", fig.height=4}
distances_plot <- seq(0, 1500, by = 10)

if (is.infinite(theta_hat)) {
  spatial_kernel_data <- expand.grid(distance = distances_plot,
                                      theta_val = c(100, 200, 500)) %>%
    mutate(
      weight = exp(-distance / theta_val),
      theta_label = paste0("θ = ", theta_val, " km")
    )

  ggplot(spatial_kernel_data, aes(x = distance, y = weight, color = theta_label)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "solid", color = "#c0392b", linewidth = 1.2) +
    scale_color_viridis_d(option = "plasma", end = 0.8, name = "Alternative\nkernels") +
    labs(x = "Distance (km)", y = "Relative Weight",
         title = "Spatial Kernels: Optimal vs. Alternatives",
         subtitle = "Optimal model (θ = ∞) shown as flat line at weight = 1") +
    annotate("text", x = 1200, y = 0.95, label = "θ = ∞ (optimal)",
             color = "#c0392b", size = 3.5, fontface = "bold") +
    theme_paper
} else {
  spatial_kernel_data <- data.frame(
    distance = distances_plot,
    weight = exp(-distances_plot / theta_hat)
  )

  ggplot(spatial_kernel_data, aes(x = distance, y = weight)) +
    geom_line(color = "#c0392b", linewidth = 1.2) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = exp(-1), linetype = "dotted", color = "gray50") +
    geom_vline(xintercept = theta_hat, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray50") +
    annotate("text", x = theta_hat + 50, y = 0.5,
             label = sprintf("θ = %d km", theta_hat),
             hjust = 0, color = "#c0392b", size = 3.5) +
    annotate("text", x = 1000, y = exp(-1) + 0.05,
             label = "37% weight",
             color = "gray40", size = 3) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(0, 250, 500, 750, 1000, 1250, 1500)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1.05), expand = c(0, 0)) +
    labs(x = "Distance (km)", y = "Relative Weight",
         title = sprintf("Estimated Spatial Decay Kernel: θ = %d km", theta_hat),
         subtitle = "Weight on protests in other districts as function of distance") +
    theme_paper
}
```

Figure 3 displays the estimated spatial kernel. `r ifelse(is.infinite(theta_hat), "With θ = ∞, all districts receive equal weight regardless of distance (flat line at 1). The alternative curves show how finite θ values would concentrate influence locally. The data reject these alternatives in favor of uniform national weighting.", paste0("The exponential decay concentrates influence locally. At distance θ = ", theta_hat, " km, weight drops to 37% of its maximum. By distance 2θ = ", 2*theta_hat, " km, weight drops to 14%. Districts beyond ", 3*theta_hat, " km exert minimal influence."))`

### Geographic Distribution of Diffusion Effects

While the preceding analysis establishes that diffusion occurs and how it decays over distance, it does not reveal where diffusion matters most. Figure 4 maps the average triggered intensity across districts, showing geographic heterogeneity in susceptibility to cross-district spillovers.

```{r figure-diffusion-map, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Geographic distribution of diffusion effects. Districts are colored by their average triggered protest intensity (expected protests attributable to cross-district contagion). Darker colors indicate districts where diffusion contributes more to local protest activity.", fig.height=6}
# Aggregate triggered intensity by district
district_diffusion <- panel_best %>%
  group_by(admin2) %>%
  summarise(
    total_triggered = sum(lambda_triggered),
    mean_triggered = mean(lambda_triggered),
    total_background = sum(lambda_bg),
    diffusion_ratio = sum(lambda_triggered) / (sum(lambda_bg) + 1e-6),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Join directly on NAME_2 since panel_best$admin2 comes from NAME_2
gadm_plot <- gadm %>%
  left_join(district_diffusion, by = c("NAME_2" = "admin2"))

# Choropleth map
ggplot(gadm_plot) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = mean_triggered), color = "white", linewidth = 0.05) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma",
                       name = "Mean Triggered\nIntensity",
                       na.value = "gray90") +
  labs(title = "Where Does Diffusion Matter Most?",
       subtitle = "Average triggered protest intensity by district, 2015-2024") +
  theme_paper +
  theme(axis.text = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        panel.grid = element_blank(),
        legend.position = "right")
```

```{r diffusion-map-stats, include=FALSE}
# Calculate summary statistics for interpretation
top_districts <- district_diffusion %>%
  arrange(desc(mean_triggered)) %>%
  head(10)

# Identify if Java districts dominate
java_provinces <- c("Banten", "DKI Jakarta", "West Java", "Central Java",
                    "DI Yogyakarta", "East Java")
```

The map reveals substantial geographic heterogeneity in diffusion effects. Districts with higher triggered intensity tend to cluster in areas with greater overall protest activity, particularly on Java and on Papua. This pattern is expected: diffusion amplifies existing protest patterns rather than redistributing them. Districts that experience many protests also generate more exposure for their neighbors, creating feedback loops that concentrate diffusion effects in already-active regions. In short, urban Java is prone to protests and these protests tend to trigger other protests in Java but not elsewhere while grievance-ridden Papua also has a higher rate of protests but primarily trigger protests elsewhere in Papua not in the rest of Indonesia. 

## Model Assessment and Diagnostics

Before turning to heterogeneity in diffusion effects, I assess model adequacy through two diagnostics: residual spatial autocorrelation and potential confounding with national shocks.

### Residual Spatial Autocorrelation

If the contagion specification is correct, model residuals should not exhibit spatial clustering. I test this using Moran's I statistic on district-averaged residuals. Moran's I measures the correlation between residuals in nearby districts. Under the null hypothesis of no spatial autocorrelation, Moran's I should be close to zero. Significant positive values indicate that the model leaves unexplained spatial structure.

```{r moran-results, echo=FALSE}
moran_table <- data.frame(
  Statistic = c("Moran's I", "Expected value", "Variance", "Z-score", "p-value"),
  Value = c(round(moran_result$estimate["Moran I statistic"], 4),
            round(moran_result$estimate["Expectation"], 4),
            round(moran_result$estimate["Variance"], 6),
            round(moran_result$statistic, 3),
            round(moran_result$p.value, 4))
)

kable(moran_table, caption = "Moran's I Test for Residual Spatial Autocorrelation",
      col.names = c("Statistic", "Value"),
      align = c("l", "r")) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position"))
```

Table 4 presents the Moran's I test results. The estimated Moran's I is `r round(moran_i, 3)` with a p-value of `r round(moran_p, 3)`. `r ifelse(moran_p < 0.05, "This indicates statistically significant residual spatial autocorrelation. The contagion model does not fully capture all spatial structure in the data. This could reflect omitted spatially-correlated variables, misspecification of the spatial kernel, or other forms of spatial dependence not captured by the cross-district exposure term. Standard errors from the quasi-Poisson model may understate true uncertainty, and coefficient estimates should be interpreted with appropriate caution.", "This indicates no statistically significant residual spatial autocorrelation. The contagion model appears to adequately capture the spatial structure in the data. Residuals in nearby districts are no more similar than expected by chance, suggesting that the cross-district exposure term successfully accounts for spatial dependence in protest occurrence.")`

### Model Specification: Count vs. Binary

Because ACLED geocodes events at the district level and the 7-day collapsing window merges nearby events, the district-day count variable is effectively binary: `r pct_binary`% of non-zero observations have a count of exactly 1, with only `r format(n_two_plus, big.mark=",")` district-days recording two or more events. To confirm that the count framing does not drive results, I re-estimate the main specification as a logistic regression with a binary outcome $\mathbb{1}(Y_{tr} > 0)$.

```{r logistic-table, echo=FALSE}
logit_table <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Quasi-Poisson (main)", "Logistic"),
  Scale = c("log-rate", "log-odds"),
  Coefficient = c(round(alpha_cross, 4), round(alpha_logit, 4)),
  SE = c(round(se_cross, 4), round(se_logit, 4)),
  `p-value` = c(format.pval(p_cross, digits = 3), format.pval(p_logit, digits = 3)),
  check.names = FALSE
)

kable(logit_table, caption = "Cross-District Exposure Coefficient: Quasi-Poisson vs. Logistic Specification",
      align = c("l", "l", "r", "r", "r")) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position"))
```

The coefficients are on different scales, log-rate for the quasi-Poisson model and log-odds for the logistic model,so their magnitudes are not directly comparable. However, the cross-district exposure coefficient is positive and statistically significant under both specifications, confirming that the contagion finding is not an artifact of the count model choice. 

```{r separate-decay-search, cache=TRUE, include=FALSE}
# ==============================================================================
# SEPARATE DECAY PARAMETER ESTIMATION
# ==============================================================================
# Estimate optimal (theta, kappa) separately for fatal and non-fatal exposure

library(future)
library(future.apply)

# Use coarser grid for computational tractability
theta_grid_sep <- c(100, 300, 500, 1000, Inf)
kappa_grid_sep <- c(0.8, 0.9, 0.95, 0.99, 1.0)

# Pre-compute spatial weight matrices
W_norm_list_sep <- lapply(theta_grid_sep, function(theta) {
  compute_spatial_weights(theta, dist_matrix)
})
names(W_norm_list_sep) <- as.character(theta_grid_sep)

param_grid_sep <- expand.grid(
  theta_idx = seq_along(theta_grid_sep),
  kappa_idx = seq_along(kappa_grid_sep)
)

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Grid search for FATAL exposure only
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
plan(multisession, workers = parallel::detectCores() - 1)

results_fatal_list <- future_lapply(seq_len(nrow(param_grid_sep)), function(i) {
  theta_idx <- param_grid_sep$theta_idx[i]
  kappa_idx <- param_grid_sep$kappa_idx[i]
  theta <- theta_grid_sep[theta_idx]
  kappa <- kappa_grid_sep[kappa_idx]
  W_norm <- W_norm_list_sep[[theta_idx]]

  cross_exp <- compute_cross_exposure_fast(W_norm, kappa, count_matrix_fatal, n_days, n_dist, max_lag = 30)

  exp_long <- data.frame(
    date = rep(dates_vec, n_dist),
    admin2 = rep(colnames(count_matrix), each = n_days),
    cross_fatal_only = as.vector(cross_exp)
  )

  panel_fit <- panel_clean %>%
    left_join(exp_long, by = c("admin2", "date"))

  m <- bam(count ~ poverty_rate + cpi +
             s(admin2_factor, bs = "re") +
             prov_year +
             national_daily +
             cross_fatal_only +
             offset(log_pop),
           data = panel_fit,
           family = poisson(link = "log"),
           discrete = TRUE)

  list(
    theta = theta,
    kappa = kappa,
    deviance = deviance(m),
    alpha = coef(m)["cross_fatal_only"],
    se = sqrt(diag(vcov(m)))["cross_fatal_only"]
  )
}, future.seed = TRUE)

plan(sequential)

results_fatal_2d <- bind_rows(lapply(results_fatal_list, as.data.frame))
best_idx_fatal <- which.min(results_fatal_2d$deviance)
theta_fatal_hat <- results_fatal_2d$theta[best_idx_fatal]
kappa_fatal_hat <- results_fatal_2d$kappa[best_idx_fatal]

# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Grid search for NON-FATAL exposure only
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
plan(multisession, workers = parallel::detectCores() - 1)

results_nonfatal_list <- future_lapply(seq_len(nrow(param_grid_sep)), function(i) {
  theta_idx <- param_grid_sep$theta_idx[i]
  kappa_idx <- param_grid_sep$kappa_idx[i]
  theta <- theta_grid_sep[theta_idx]
  kappa <- kappa_grid_sep[kappa_idx]
  W_norm <- W_norm_list_sep[[theta_idx]]

  cross_exp <- compute_cross_exposure_fast(W_norm, kappa, count_matrix_nonfatal, n_days, n_dist, max_lag = 30)

  exp_long <- data.frame(
    date = rep(dates_vec, n_dist),
    admin2 = rep(colnames(count_matrix), each = n_days),
    cross_nonfatal_only = as.vector(cross_exp)
  )

  panel_fit <- panel_clean %>%
    left_join(exp_long, by = c("admin2", "date"))

  m <- bam(count ~ poverty_rate + cpi +
             s(admin2_factor, bs = "re") +
             prov_year +
             national_daily +
             cross_nonfatal_only +
             offset(log_pop),
           data = panel_fit,
           family = poisson(link = "log"),
           discrete = TRUE)

  list(
    theta = theta,
    kappa = kappa,
    deviance = deviance(m),
    alpha = coef(m)["cross_nonfatal_only"],
    se = sqrt(diag(vcov(m)))["cross_nonfatal_only"]
  )
}, future.seed = TRUE)

plan(sequential)

results_nonfatal_2d <- bind_rows(lapply(results_nonfatal_list, as.data.frame))
best_idx_nonfatal <- which.min(results_nonfatal_2d$deviance)
theta_nonfatal_hat <- results_nonfatal_2d$theta[best_idx_nonfatal]
kappa_nonfatal_hat <- results_nonfatal_2d$kappa[best_idx_nonfatal]
```

```{r separate-decay-model, cache=TRUE, include=FALSE}
# ==============================================================================
# FIT MODEL WITH TYPE-SPECIFIC DECAY PARAMETERS
# ==============================================================================

# Compute exposure terms with type-specific optimal parameters
W_fatal_opt <- compute_spatial_weights(theta_fatal_hat, dist_matrix)
W_nonfatal_opt <- compute_spatial_weights(theta_nonfatal_hat, dist_matrix)

cross_exp_fatal_sep <- compute_cross_exposure_fast(
  W_fatal_opt, kappa_fatal_hat, count_matrix_fatal, n_days, n_dist, max_lag = 30
)
cross_exp_nonfatal_sep <- compute_cross_exposure_fast(
  W_nonfatal_opt, kappa_nonfatal_hat, count_matrix_nonfatal, n_days, n_dist, max_lag = 30
)

# Merge into panel
exp_sep_long <- data.frame(
  date = rep(dates_vec, n_dist),
  admin2 = rep(colnames(count_matrix), each = n_days),
  cross_fatal_sep = as.vector(cross_exp_fatal_sep),
  cross_nonfatal_sep = as.vector(cross_exp_nonfatal_sep)
)

panel_sep <- panel_best %>%
  left_join(exp_sep_long, by = c("admin2", "date"))

# Fit model with separate decay parameters
m_sep <- bam(count ~ poverty_rate + cpi +
               s(admin2_factor, bs = "re") +
               prov_year +
               national_daily +
               cross_fatal_sep + cross_nonfatal_sep +
               offset(log_pop),
             data = panel_sep,
             family = quasipoisson(link = "log"),
             discrete = TRUE)

# Extract coefficients
alpha_fatal_sep <- coef(m_sep)["cross_fatal_sep"]
alpha_nonfatal_sep <- coef(m_sep)["cross_nonfatal_sep"]
vcov_sep <- vcov(m_sep)
se_fatal_sep <- sqrt(vcov_sep["cross_fatal_sep", "cross_fatal_sep"])
se_nonfatal_sep <- sqrt(vcov_sep["cross_nonfatal_sep", "cross_nonfatal_sep"])
cov_sep <- vcov_sep["cross_fatal_sep", "cross_nonfatal_sep"]

# Wald test for coefficient equality
diff_sep <- alpha_fatal_sep - alpha_nonfatal_sep
se_diff_sep <- sqrt(se_fatal_sep^2 + se_nonfatal_sep^2 - 2 * cov_sep)
z_sep <- diff_sep / se_diff_sep
p_sep <- 2 * pnorm(-abs(z_sep))

# Compute half-lives
half_life_fatal <- if (kappa_fatal_hat < 1) log(0.5) / log(kappa_fatal_hat) else Inf
half_life_nonfatal <- if (kappa_nonfatal_hat < 1) log(0.5) / log(kappa_nonfatal_hat) else Inf
```

## Heterogeneous Diffusion: The Martyrdom Effect

The preceding analysis treats all protests as equivalent triggers for diffusion. However, the theory motivating this study suggests that event characteristics, particularly severity, should shape both the magnitude and the spatial-temporal structure of spillovers. This section investigates heterogeneous diffusion by event type, first asking whether fatal and non-fatal protests diffuse through different channels, then testing whether fatal protests generate stronger spillovers.

### Do Fatal and Non-Fatal Protests Diffuse Differently?

Fatal events may spread nationally through mass media regardless of geographic distance, while non-fatal protests might diffuse primarily to nearby districts through local networks and regional media markets. Similarly, the temporal persistence of spillovers might differ: fatal events that generate sustained media coverage and moral outrage may trigger mobilization over longer periods than routine protests that fade quickly from public attention.

To investigate this, I estimate separate optimal decay parameters for fatal exposure ($\theta_F$, $\kappa_F$) and non-fatal exposure ($\theta_N$, $\kappa_N$) using the same grid search procedure as the main analysis.

```{r table-decay-params, echo=FALSE}
decay_table <- data.frame(
  Parameter = c("Spatial decay θ (km)", "Temporal decay κ", "Half-life (days)"),
  Fatal = c(
    ifelse(is.infinite(theta_fatal_hat), "∞", format(theta_fatal_hat, big.mark = ",")),
    format(round(kappa_fatal_hat, 3), nsmall = 3),
    ifelse(is.infinite(half_life_fatal), "∞", format(round(half_life_fatal, 1), nsmall = 1))
  ),
  `Non-Fatal` = c(
    ifelse(is.infinite(theta_nonfatal_hat), "∞", format(theta_nonfatal_hat, big.mark = ",")),
    format(round(kappa_nonfatal_hat, 3), nsmall = 3),
    ifelse(is.infinite(half_life_nonfatal), "∞", format(round(half_life_nonfatal, 1), nsmall = 1))
  ),
  Pooled = c(
    ifelse(is.infinite(theta_hat), "∞", format(theta_hat, big.mark = ",")),
    format(round(kappa_hat, 3), nsmall = 3),
    ifelse(is.infinite(half_life), "∞", format(round(half_life, 1), nsmall = 1))
  )
)

kable(decay_table,
      caption = "Decay Parameters by Exposure Type",
      col.names = c("Parameter", "Fatal", "Non-Fatal", "Pooled (Main)"),
      align = c("l", "r", "r", "r")) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position")) %>%
  footnote(general = "Parameters estimated via grid search. Half-life = days for exposure weight to halve.",
           general_title = "Note: ",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
```

```{r decay-interpretation, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
theta_diff <- theta_fatal_hat != theta_nonfatal_hat
kappa_diff <- abs(kappa_fatal_hat - kappa_nonfatal_hat) > 0.01

if (theta_diff & kappa_diff) {
  cat("The results reveal that fatal and non-fatal protests exhibit markedly different diffusion patterns in *both* spatial reach and temporal persistence. ")
} else if (theta_diff) {
  cat("The results reveal that fatal and non-fatal protests exhibit different spatial diffusion patterns. ")
} else if (kappa_diff) {
  cat("The results reveal that fatal and non-fatal protests exhibit different temporal persistence. ")
} else {
  cat("The results suggest similar decay structures for both event types. ")
}

if (is.infinite(theta_fatal_hat) & !is.infinite(theta_nonfatal_hat)) {
  cat(sprintf("Fatal protests diffuse nationally regardless of distance (θ_F = ∞), while non-fatal protests show localized diffusion with effects concentrated among nearby districts (θ_N = %s km). ", format(theta_nonfatal_hat, big.mark = ",")))
} else if (!is.infinite(theta_fatal_hat) & is.infinite(theta_nonfatal_hat)) {
  cat(sprintf("Fatal protests show localized diffusion (θ_F = %s km), while non-fatal protests diffuse more broadly (θ_N = ∞). ", format(theta_fatal_hat, big.mark = ",")))
} else if (theta_fatal_hat > theta_nonfatal_hat) {
  cat(sprintf("Fatal protests diffuse over greater distances (θ_F = %s km vs. θ_N = %s km). ",
              ifelse(is.infinite(theta_fatal_hat), "∞", format(theta_fatal_hat, big.mark = ",")),
              ifelse(is.infinite(theta_nonfatal_hat), "∞", format(theta_nonfatal_hat, big.mark = ","))))
}

if (kappa_fatal_hat > kappa_nonfatal_hat) {
  cat(sprintf("Fatal protests also show greater temporal persistence (κ_F = %.2f vs. κ_N = %.2f), with half-lives of %s vs. %s days respectively.",
              kappa_fatal_hat, kappa_nonfatal_hat,
              ifelse(is.infinite(half_life_fatal), "∞", round(half_life_fatal, 1)),
              ifelse(is.infinite(half_life_nonfatal), "∞", round(half_life_nonfatal, 1))))
} else if (kappa_fatal_hat < kappa_nonfatal_hat) {
  cat(sprintf("Non-fatal protests show greater temporal persistence (κ_N = %.2f vs. κ_F = %.2f), with half-lives of %s vs. %s days respectively.",
              kappa_nonfatal_hat, kappa_fatal_hat,
              ifelse(is.infinite(half_life_nonfatal), "∞", round(half_life_nonfatal, 1)),
              ifelse(is.infinite(half_life_fatal), "∞", round(half_life_fatal, 1))))
}
```

These differences have substantive implications. Fatal protests, which receive national media coverage and provoke widespread moral outrage, trigger action regardless of geographic proximity. Non-fatal protests, by contrast, spread primarily through local networks, personal contacts, and regional media markets that attenuate with distance.

### Do Fatal Protests Trigger Stronger Spillovers?

Given that fatal and non-fatal protests diffuse through different spatial-temporal structures, the natural next question is whether fatal protests also generate *stronger* spillovers. The martyrdom hypothesis predicts that protests involving fatalities should produce larger downstream effects: deaths attract disproportionate media attention, provoke moral outrage, and create focal points for collective action.

To test this while respecting the different decay structures identified above, I estimate a model that allows each exposure type to use its own optimal decay parameters. This provides a cleaner comparison than imposing common decay on processes that operate at different scales. For reference, I also report results from a model with pooled decay parameters.

```{r table-separate-model, echo=FALSE}
model_table <- data.frame(
  Model = c("Pooled decay parameters", "Separate decay parameters"),
  `Fatal Coef` = c(
    sprintf("%.3f (%.3f)", alpha_fatal, se_fatal),
    sprintf("%.3f (%.3f)", alpha_fatal_sep, se_fatal_sep)
  ),
  `Non-Fatal Coef` = c(
    sprintf("%.3f (%.3f)", alpha_nonfatal, se_nonfatal),
    sprintf("%.3f (%.3f)", alpha_nonfatal_sep, se_nonfatal_sep)
  ),
  `Difference` = c(
    sprintf("%.3f (%.3f)", diff_fatal, se_diff_fatal),
    sprintf("%.3f (%.3f)", diff_sep, se_diff_sep)
  ),
  `p-value` = c(
    ifelse(p_fatal < 0.001, "< 0.001", format(round(p_fatal, 4), nsmall = 4)),
    ifelse(p_sep < 0.001, "< 0.001", format(round(p_sep, 4), nsmall = 4))
  )
)

kable(model_table,
      caption = "The Martyrdom Effect: Fatal vs. Non-Fatal Protest Spillovers",
      col.names = c("Decay Parameters", "Fatal α", "Non-Fatal α", "Difference", "p-value"),
      align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r")) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position")) %>%
  footnote(general = "Standard errors in parentheses. p-value tests H0: α_fatal = α_nonfatal.",
           general_title = "Note: ",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
```

```{r martyrdom-interpretation, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
if (p_sep < 0.05 & diff_sep > 0) {
  cat(sprintf("Using type-specific decay parameters, the coefficient on fatal protest exposure ($\\hat{\\alpha}_F$ = %.3f) is significantly larger than the coefficient on non-fatal exposure ($\\hat{\\alpha}_N$ = %.3f), with a difference of %.3f (p = %.4f). Fatal protests generate stronger spillovers than non-fatal protests, consistent with the martyrdom hypothesis.", alpha_fatal_sep, alpha_nonfatal_sep, diff_sep, p_sep))
} else if (p_sep < 0.05 & diff_sep < 0) {
  cat(sprintf("Using type-specific decay parameters, the coefficient on non-fatal protest exposure ($\\hat{\\alpha}_N$ = %.3f) is significantly larger than the coefficient on fatal exposure ($\\hat{\\alpha}_F$ = %.3f), with p = %.4f. This is the opposite of what the martyrdom hypothesis predicts.", alpha_nonfatal_sep, alpha_fatal_sep, p_sep))
} else if (p_sep >= 0.05) {
  cat(sprintf("Using type-specific decay parameters, the difference between fatal ($\\hat{\\alpha}_F$ = %.3f) and non-fatal ($\\hat{\\alpha}_N$ = %.3f) coefficients is not statistically significant (p = %.4f). While fatal and non-fatal protests diffuse through different spatial-temporal structures, their overall capacity to trigger subsequent protests is similar in magnitude.", alpha_fatal_sep, alpha_nonfatal_sep, p_sep))
}
```

Taken together, these results reveal that protest diffusion is more nuanced than a single coefficient can capture. Fatal and non-fatal protests differ in their spatial reach, with fatal protests spreading nationally while non-fatal protests diffuse locally. Future work might explore the mechanisms underlying these differences, whether through media markets, social networks, or other channels that vary with event severity.

\newpage

### Next Steps

I first aim to refine the framing of this method as I am concerned that I might be overconfident in the novelty of this methods and results. I need to find a better way to balance the literature review between the advantages of this method over other designs without misrepresenting the goals and methods used in other papers. There are many papers that I did not cite in the literature review that make claims about how and why protests spread without explicit investigation into protest diffusion that I do not necessarily want to criticize. The literature on cross-national protest diffusion is also fairly robust and I may need to more explicitly position my interest as in subnational diffusion and why that is an important departure. The novelty in this paper may ultimately just be in the differences in the spatial decay between fatal and non-fatal protests which might need to be given more priority.

This study intentionally excludes the possibility that protest in a location at one point in time increase the number of protests in the same location at a later point in time. I made this decision for a few reasons. First, I aim to study spatial diffusion of protests not the persistence of a given protest. Second most protest data does not distinguish between multi-day protests and independent protests reported on consecutive days which poses empirical challenges. It is also ambiguous where we should draw the line conceptually between old protests and new protests across consecutive days. My question for the committee is whether this is a defensible position or should I endeavor to better incorporate
within-location contagion across time into the model?

I used a Poisson model to estimate the conditional intensity of protests at a given district-day. I did this because it is the standard approach for Hawkes model and other point process methods. However, there are very few district-days with more than one protest which essentially means the outcome is binary. While a quasi-poisson and logistic regression should converge under these conditions to the similar values, I'd still like to confirm the findings would be substantively similar under a different modelling choice. I have done one robustness test to confirm the contagion outcome still held when using a binary outcome and a logistic regression but I'd like to redo the other parts of the paper for confidence. 


\newpage

# Appendix: Additional Figures

```{r figure-profile-2d, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Two-dimensional profile likelihood over spatial (θ) and temporal (κ) decay parameters. Darker colors indicate lower deviance (better fit). The optimal combination is marked with ×.", fig.height=5}
profile_2d <- results_2d %>%
  mutate(
    theta_label = ifelse(is.infinite(theta), "∞", as.character(theta)),
    theta_label = factor(theta_label,
                         levels = c("50", "100", "200", "300", "500", "750", "1000", "1500", "∞")),
    kappa_label = sprintf("%.2f", kappa),
    kappa_label = factor(kappa_label, levels = sprintf("%.2f", sort(unique(kappa)))),
    is_optimal = (theta == theta_hat & kappa == kappa_hat)
  )

opt_point <- profile_2d %>% filter(is_optimal)

ggplot(profile_2d, aes(x = theta_label, y = kappa_label, fill = deviance)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
  geom_point(data = opt_point, aes(x = theta_label, y = kappa_label),
             color = "#e74c3c", size = 4, shape = 4, stroke = 1.5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", direction = -1, name = "Deviance") +
  labs(x = expression(paste("Spatial Decay ", theta, " (km)")),
       y = expression(paste("Temporal Decay ", kappa))) +
  theme_paper +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

```{r figure-timeseries, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Daily protest counts in Indonesia, 2015-2024.", fig.height=3.5}
daily_counts_plot <- protests_daily %>%
  group_by(date) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(daily_counts_plot, aes(x = date, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2c3e50", alpha = 0.7, width = 1) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0), limits = c(0, NA)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Daily Protest Count") +
  theme_paper +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

```{r figure-lag-structure, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Cross-district contagion coefficients by weekly lag window. Error bars show 95% confidence intervals.", fig.height=3.5}
lag_data <- data.frame(
  week = factor(c("Week 1\n(1-7)", "Week 2\n(8-14)", "Week 3\n(15-21)", "Week 4\n(22-30)"),
                levels = c("Week 1\n(1-7)", "Week 2\n(8-14)", "Week 3\n(15-21)", "Week 4\n(22-30)")),
  coef = coef(m_lags)[paste0("week", 1:4)],
  se = summary(m_lags)$p.table[paste0("week", 1:4), "Std. Error"]
)

ggplot(lag_data, aes(x = week, y = coef)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#3498db", alpha = 0.8, width = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = coef - 1.96*se, ymax = coef + 1.96*se),
                width = 0.2, linewidth = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  labs(x = "Lag Period", y = "Coefficient") +
  theme_paper
```

\newpage

# Appendix: Data and Sample

### Sensitivity to Event Collapse Window

The main analysis collapses multi-day protests at the same location within a 7-day window into single events. This prevents double-counting of continuous protests but involves a researcher discretion. To assess sensitivity to this choice, I re-estimate the contagion model using alternative collapse windows: 0 days (no collapsing, with each reported event treated as separate), 2, 3, and 5 days. All windows use uniform collapse logic without special treatment for any location, and all use the optimal spatial and temporal decay parameters ($\hat{\theta}$ = `r ifelse(is.infinite(theta_hat), "∞", theta_hat)` km, $\hat{\kappa}$ = `r round(kappa_hat, 2)`) from the main analysis.

```{r table-collapse-robustness, echo=FALSE}
collapse_table <- collapse_robustness_table %>%
  mutate(
    Window = paste0(gap_days, ifelse(gap_days == 1, " day", " days")),
    Window = ifelse(gap_days == 0, "None", Window),
    Events = format(n_events, big.mark = ","),
    Estimate = round(alpha, 3),
    SE = round(se, 3),
    CI = paste0("[", round(ci_lower, 3), ", ", round(ci_upper, 3), "]"),
    p = ifelse(p_value < 0.001, "< 0.001", round(p_value, 3))
  ) %>%
  select(Window, Events, Estimate, SE, CI, p)

kable(collapse_table,
      caption = "Sensitivity of Contagion Estimate to Event Collapse Window",
      col.names = c("Collapse Window", "Events", "α̂", "SE", "95% CI", "p-value"),
      align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "c", "r"),
      row.names = FALSE) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position")) %>%
  footnote(general = "All models use optimal decay parameters from main analysis.",
           general_title = "Note: ",
           footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
```

The table shows that the cross-district contagion coefficient remains `r if(all(collapse_robustness_table$p_value < 0.05)) "statistically significant" else "positive"` across all collapse window choices. The point estimates range from `r round(min(collapse_robustness_table$alpha), 3)` to `r round(max(collapse_robustness_table$alpha), 3)`, with the 7-day window (main specification) yielding an estimate of `r round(collapse_robustness_table$alpha[collapse_robustness_table$gap_days == 7], 3)`. `r if(abs(max(collapse_robustness_table$alpha) - min(collapse_robustness_table$alpha)) / mean(collapse_robustness_table$alpha) < 0.3) "The estimates are reasonably stable across windows, suggesting that the main finding is not driven by the particular collapse window choice." else "Some variation in estimates across windows suggests that the collapse decision matters for the precise magnitude, though the qualitative conclusion of positive contagion remains robust."`

```{r table-sample, echo=FALSE}
sample_table <- data.frame(
  Stage = c("Raw ACLED events",
            "Protests and riots",
            "After 7-day collapse",
            "Unique protest-location-days",
            "Districts in panel",
            "District-days in estimation sample"),
  N = c(format(n_raw_events, big.mark=","),
        format(n_protest_events, big.mark=","),
        format(n_collapsed, big.mark=","),
        format(n_daily, big.mark=","),
        format(n_districts, big.mark=","),
        format(n_estimation_sample, big.mark=","))
)

kable(sample_table, caption = "Sample Construction",
      col.names = c("Stage", "N"),
      align = c("l", "r")) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = c("hold_position"))
```




```{r session-info, echo=FALSE, eval=FALSE}
sessionInfo()
```


\newpage

# References
